perturbation.original_text: Each party shall exercise such care in the protection of the confidential information of the other as they exercise in the protection of confidential information of their own.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall exercise reasonable care in the protection of the confidential information of the other.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'as they exercise in the protection of confidential information of their own' weakens the standard of care required, potentially violating laws in some jurisdictions (e.g., California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act) that require companies to take active steps to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Removing the comparison to 'their own' level of care implies a lower standard, making it easier to claim inadequate protection under the law.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text requires each party to protect confidential information of the other with the same level of care they use to protect their own. The changed text weakens this by only requiring 'reasonable care'. The Perturbation Explanation states this may violate laws in some jurisdictions (e.g., California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act) that require companies to take active steps to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and removing the comparison to 'their own' level of care implies a lower standard. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426, establishes the existence of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in California, strengthening the argument that weakening the protection of confidential information could lead to a legal issue in that state.

perturbation.original_text: Phoenix and Company warrant that they have the unqualified right to disclose fully the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Phoenix and Company make no warranties regarding their right to disclose the Confidential Information disclosed hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By disclaiming any warranty of the right to disclose, the contract potentially violates laws that impose liability for disclosing information without proper authorization or violating non-disclosure agreements with third parties. This is particularly relevant under trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: DTSA provides a cause of action for misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the warranty, a party disclosing confidential information without proper rights avoids liability under the agreement, but it does not exempt them from liabilities under the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The original text contains a warranty that Phoenix and Company have the right to disclose confidential information. The changed text removes this warranty, explicitly stating they make no warranties. The Law Explanation states this removal avoids liability under the agreement but does not exempt them from liabilities under the DTSA (presumably the Defend Trade Secrets Act). Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, discusses civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets, stating that an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action. By removing the warranty, Phoenix and Company attempt to shield themselves from liability within the contract, but this does not protect them from external laws regarding trade secrets. This omission of warranty directly contradicts the legal protections afforded to trade secret owners, making the change a significant legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: At the termination of this examination, both parties agree to return to each other all of the documents and other information provided in connection with this examination, and all copies thereof, as soon as requested by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: At the termination of this examination, both parties agree to return the documents and other information provided in connection with this examination.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'all copies thereof, as soon as requested by the other party' significantly weakens the obligation to return confidential information, potentially violating legal requirements related to the destruction or return of trade secrets upon termination of an agreement. Some regulations specify a specific timeframe.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the EEA focuses on theft, the failure to return 'all copies' could be construed as an attempt to retain trade secret information unlawfully, especially if there's a deliberate attempt to conceal those copies. The failure to return all copies upon request increases the likelihood of legal action.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the phrase 'all copies thereof, as soon as requested by the other party'. The original text ensures that all documents and their copies are returned upon request. The omission weakens the obligation to return all copies, potentially allowing a party to retain copies of trade secrets. 18 USC 1832 addresses the theft of trade secrets, and retaining copies could be construed as an attempt to convert trade secret information, especially if there is a deliberate attempt to conceal those copies. The failure to return all copies upon request increases the likelihood of legal action related to trade secret misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Phoenix and Company agree to maintain as Confidential Information, the existence of these discussions regarding a possible business relationship, until an agreement is completed.
perturbation.changed_text: Phoenix and Company may disclose the existence of these discussions regarding a possible business relationship.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to maintain confidentiality regarding the existence of discussions can violate securities laws or regulations in certain circumstances, especially if one of the parties is a publicly traded company. Premature disclosure of negotiations could be considered insider information or market manipulation. The original text guaranteed it until an agreement was completed.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Premature disclosure of business discussions could constitute such a device if it is intended to manipulate the market or if it involves insider information used for trading purposes. While a direct breach is not guaranteed, the permission to disclose opens the door to potential violations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry | Investor.gov Skip to main content The .gov means it’s official. Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. The site is secure. The https:// ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. Investor.gov U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission Search Investor.gov The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry Note: Except as otherwise noted, the links to the securities laws below are from Statute Compilations maintained by the Office of the Legislative Counsel, U.S. House of Representatives. These links are provided for the user's convenience and may not reflect all recent amendments. If you have questions concerning the meaning or application of a particular law, please consult with an attorney who specializes in securities law. Securities Act of 1933 Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Trust Indenture Act of 1939 Investment Company Act of 1940 Investment Advisers Act of 1940 Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010 Jumpstart Our Business Startups Act of 2012 Rules and Regulations Securities Act of 1933 Often referred to as the "truth in securities" law, the Securities Act of 1933 has two basic objectives: require that investors receive financial and other significant information concerning securities being offered for public sale; and prohibit deceit, misrepresentations, and other fraud in the sale of securities. See the full text of the Securities Act of 1933 . Purpose of Registration A primary means of accomplishing these goals is the disclosure of important financial information through the registration of securities. This information enables investors, not the government, to make informed judgments about whether to purchase a company's securities. While the SEC requires that the information provided be accurate, it does not guarantee it. Investors who purchase securities and suffer losses have important recovery rights if they can prove that there was incomplete or inaccurate disclosure of important information. The Registration Process In general, securities sold in the U.S. must be registered. The registration forms companies file provide essential facts while minimizing the burden and expense of complying with the law. In general, registration forms call for: a description of the company's properties and business; a description of the security to be offered for sale; information about the management of the company; and financial statements certified by independent accountants. Registration statements and prospectuses become public shortly after filing with the SEC. If filed by U.S. domestic companies, the statements are available on the EDGAR database accessible at www.sec.gov. Registration statements are subject to examination for compliance with disclosure requirements. Not all offerings of securities must be registered with the Commission. Some exemptions from the registration requirement include: private offerings to a limited number of persons or institutions; offerings of limited size; intrastate offerings; and securities of municipal, state, and federal governments. By exempting many small offerings from the registration process, the SEC seeks to foster capital formation by lowering the cost of offering securities to the public. Securities Exchange Act of 1934 With this Act, Congress created the Securities and Exchange Commission. The Act empowers the SEC with broad authority over all aspects of the securities industry. This includes the power to register, regulate, and oversee brokerage firms, transfer agents, and clearing agencies as well as the nation's securities self regulatory organizations (SROs). The various securities exchanges, such as the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ Stock Market, and the Chicago Board of Options are SROs. The
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates confidentiality regarding business discussions until an agreement is reached, while the changed text permits disclosure. This contradiction has the potential to violate securities laws, as premature disclosure could be seen as market manipulation or involve insider information used for trading, as explained by the LLM. The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 empowers the SEC with broad authority over all aspects of the securities industry, according to Scraped Law Snippet 1, including the power to regulate and oversee brokerage firms. Allowing disclosure, where it was previously forbidden, opens the door for violations in the securities industry.
